Problem Statement¶

Business Context¶

Renewable energy sources play an increasingly important role in the global energy mix, as the effort to reduce the environmental impact of energy production increases.

Out of all the renewable energy alternatives, wind energy is one of the most developed technologies worldwide. The U.S Department of Energy has put together a guide to achieving operational efficiency using predictive maintenance practices.

Predictive maintenance uses sensor information and analysis methods to measure and predict degradation and future component capability. The idea behind predictive maintenance is that failure patterns are predictable and if component failure can be predicted accurately and the component is replaced before it fails, the costs of operation and maintenance will be much lower.

The sensors fitted across different machines involved in the process of energy generation collect data related to various environmental factors (temperature, humidity, wind speed, etc.) and additional features related to various parts of the wind turbine (gearbox, tower, blades, break, etc.).

Objective¶

“ReneWind” is a company working on improving the machinery/processes involved in the production of wind energy using machine learning and has collected data of generator failure of wind turbines using sensors. They have shared a ciphered version of the data, as the data collected through sensors is confidential (the type of data collected varies with companies). Data has 40 predictors, 20000 observations in the training set and 5000 in the test set.

The objective is to build various classification models, tune them, and find the best one that will help identify failures so that the generators could be repaired before failing/breaking to reduce the overall maintenance cost. The nature of predictions made by the classification model will translate as follows:

  • True positives (TP) are failures correctly predicted by the model. These will result in repairing costs.
  • False negatives (FN) are real failures where there is no detection by the model. These will result in replacement costs.
  • False positives (FP) are detections where there is no failure. These will result in inspection costs.

It is given that the cost of repairing a generator is much less than the cost of replacing it, and the cost of inspection is less than the cost of repair.

“1” in the target variables should be considered as “failure” and “0” represents “No failure”.

Data Description¶

The data provided is a transformed version of the original data which was collected using sensors.

  • Train.csv - To be used for training and tuning of models.
  • Test.csv - To be used only for testing the performance of the final best model.

Both the datasets consist of 40 predictor variables and 1 target variable.

Installing and Importing the necessary libraries¶

In [218]:
# Installing the libraries with the specified version
!pip install --no-deps tensorflow==2.18.0 scikit-learn==1.3.2 matplotlib===3.8.3 seaborn==0.13.2 numpy==1.26.4 pandas==2.2.2 -q --user --no-warn-script-location

Note:

  • After running the above cell, kindly restart the runtime (for Google Colab) or notebook kernel (for Jupyter Notebook), and run all cells sequentially from the next cell.
  • On executing the above line of code, you might see a warning regarding package dependencies. This error message can be ignored as the above code ensures that all necessary libraries and their dependencies are maintained to successfully execute the code in this notebook.
In [219]:
# Library for data manipulation and analysis.
import pandas as pd
# Fundamental package for scientific computing.
import numpy as np
#splitting datasets into training and testing sets.
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
#Imports tools for data preprocessing including label encoding, one-hot encoding, and standard scaling
from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelEncoder, OneHotEncoder,StandardScaler
#Imports a class for imputing missing values in datasets.
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer
#Imports the Matplotlib library for creating visualizations.
from sklearn.metrics import confusion_matrix
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
# Imports the Seaborn library for statistical data visualization.
import seaborn as sns
# Time related functions.
import time
#Imports functions for evaluating the performance of machine learning models
from sklearn.metrics import confusion_matrix, f1_score,accuracy_score, recall_score, precision_score, classification_report
#Imports metrics from
from sklearn import metrics

#Imports the tensorflow,keras and layers.
import tensorflow
import tensorflow as tf
from tensorflow.keras.models import Sequential
from tensorflow.keras.layers import Dense
from tensorflow.keras.layers import Dense, Input, Dropout,BatchNormalization
from tensorflow.keras import backend

# to suppress unnecessary warnings
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

Loading the Data¶

In [220]:
# uncomment and run the following lines for Google Colab
from google.colab import drive
drive.mount('/content/drive')

Drive already mounted at /content/drive; to attempt to forcibly remount, call drive.mount("/content/drive", force_remount=True).
In [221]:
data_train = pd.read_csv('/content/drive/MyDrive/Colab Notebooks/data/Neural Networks/Train.csv')
data_test = pd.read_csv('/content/drive/MyDrive/Colab Notebooks/data/Neural Networks/Test.csv')

renewind_train = data_train.copy()
renewind_test = data_test.copy()

Observations:¶

Both in the training and testing dataset the distribution of all 40 features is pretty balanced. Nothing insightful can be detected from this statistical summary

Exploratory Data Analysis¶

Univariate analysis¶

In [235]:
def histogram_boxplot(data, feature, figsize=(15, 10), kde=False, bins=None):
    """
    Boxplot and histogram combined

    data: dataframe
    feature: dataframe column
    figsize: size of figure (default (15,10))
    kde: whether to show the density curve (default False)
    bins: number of bins for histogram (default None)
    """
    f2, (ax_box2, ax_hist2) = plt.subplots(
        nrows=2,  # Number of rows of the subplot grid= 2
        sharex=True,  # x-axis will be shared among all subplots
        gridspec_kw={"height_ratios": (0.25, 0.75)},
        figsize=figsize,
    )  # creating the 2 subplots
    sns.boxplot(
        data=data, x=feature, ax=ax_box2, showmeans=True, color="violet"
    )  # boxplot will be created and a triangle will indicate the mean value of the column
    ax_box2.set_title(f'Boxplot of {feature}') # Add title to boxplot
    ax_box2.set_xlabel(feature) # Add x-label to boxplot
    sns.histplot(
        data=data, x=feature, kde=kde, ax=ax_hist2, bins=bins
    ) if bins else sns.histplot(
        data=data, x=feature, kde=kde, ax=ax_hist2
    )  # For histogram
    ax_hist2.axvline(
        data[feature].mean(), color="green", linestyle="--"
    )  # Add mean to the histogram
    ax_hist2.axvline(
        data[feature].median(), color="black", linestyle="-"
    )  # Add median to the histogram
    ax_hist2.set_title(f'Histogram of {feature}') # Add title to histogram
    ax_hist2.set_xlabel(feature) # Add x-label to histogram
    plt.show() # Display the plot

Variables V1 to V40¶

In [236]:
for feature in renewind_train.columns:
    histogram_boxplot(renewind_train, feature, figsize=(12, 7), kde=False, bins=None)

Observations:¶

  • Most of the variables appear to be symmetrically distributed around their means, with some exceptions exhibiting skewness and potential outliers as seen in the boxplots.
  • Variables V1 and V2 have a small number of missing values in both the training and test datasets, which will need to be handled during data preprocessing.

Checking the distrubution of Target variable¶

In [237]:
# For train data
target_counts_train = renewind_train["Target"].value_counts()
target_proportions_train = renewind_train["Target"].value_counts(normalize=True)

print("Target Variable Distribution (Train Data):")
print("Counts:")
print(target_counts_train)
print("\nPercentages:")
print(target_proportions_train)

Target Variable Distribution (Train Data):
Counts:
Target
0.0    18890
1.0     1110
Name: count, dtype: int64

Percentages:
Target
0.0    0.9445
1.0    0.0555
Name: proportion, dtype: float64
In [238]:
# For test data
target_counts_test = renewind_test["Target"].value_counts()
target_proportions_test = renewind_test["Target"].value_counts(normalize=True)

print("Target Variable Distribution (Train Data):")
print("Counts:")
print(target_counts_train)
print("\nPercentages:")
print(target_proportions_train)

Target Variable Distribution (Train Data):
Counts:
Target
0.0    18890
1.0     1110
Name: count, dtype: int64

Percentages:
Target
0.0    0.9445
1.0    0.0555
Name: proportion, dtype: float64

Observations:¶

  • The target variable is highly imbalanced in both the training and test datasets, with a significantly larger number of observations belonging to class 0 (no failure) compared to class 1 (failure).
  • This imbalance needs to be addressed during model building to prevent the model from being biased towards the majority class.

Bivariate Analysis¶

Correlation Check¶

In [239]:
cols_list = renewind_train.select_dtypes(include=np.number).columns.tolist()
cols_list.remove("Target")

plt.figure(figsize=(25, 20))
sns.heatmap(
    renewind_train[cols_list].corr(), annot=True, vmin=-1, vmax=1, fmt=".2f", cmap="Spectral",
    xticklabels='auto', yticklabels='auto'
)
plt.show()

Observations from Correlation Check:¶

  • There are many features that have both strong positive correlation and strong negative correlation.
  • Nothing conclusive can be said from this correlation matrix, since this eliminated target variables and evaluates if certain features may lead to multi-collinearity.
  • From the correlation matrix, it can se said that there are changes of some features strongly correlated and hence we must consider L1 regularization

Data Preprocessing¶

Data Preparation for Modeling¶

In [240]:
# Dividing train data into X and y
X = renewind_train.drop(columns = ["Target"] , axis=1)
y = renewind_train["Target"]

Since we already have a separate test set, we don't need to divide data into train, valiation and test

In [241]:
# Splitting data into training and validation set:

X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=1, stratify=y
)
In [242]:
# Checking the number of rows and columns in the X_train data
X_train.shape
Out[242]:
(16000, 40)
In [243]:
# Checking the number of rows and columns in the X_val data
X_val.shape
Out[243]:
(4000, 40)
In [244]:
# Dividing test data into X_test and y_test
X_test = renewind_test.drop(columns = ['Target'] , axis= 1)
y_test = renewind_test['Target']
In [245]:
# Checking the number of rows and columns in the X_test data
X_test.shape
Out[245]:
(5000, 40)

Missing Value Imputation¶

  • There were few missing values in V1 and V2, we will impute them using the median.
  • And to avoid data leakage we will impute missing values after splitting train data into train and validation sets.
In [246]:
imputer = SimpleImputer(strategy="median")
In [247]:
# Fit and transform the train data
X_train = pd.DataFrame(imputer.fit_transform(X_train), columns=X_train.columns)

# Transform the validation data
X_val = pd.DataFrame(imputer.fit_transform(X_val), columns=X_val.columns)

# Transform the test data
X_test = pd.DataFrame(imputer.fit_transform(X_test), columns=X_test.columns)
In [248]:
# Checking that no column has missing values in train or test sets
print(X_train.isna().sum())
print("-" * 30)
print(X_val.isna().sum())
print("-" * 30)
print(X_test.isna().sum())

In [249]:
y_train = y_train
y_val = y_val
y_test = y_test

Model Building¶

Model Evaluation Criterion¶

Given the problem statement, where the cost of replacing a generator (False Negative) is much higher than repairing it (True Positive) or inspecting it unnecessarily (False Positive), the most crucial model evaluation metric is Recall. Maximizing recall will ensure that the model identifies as many actual failures as possible, thereby minimizing the occurrence of costly false negatives.

Utility Functions¶

In [310]:
def plot(history, name):
    """
    Function to plot loss/accuracy

    history: an object which stores the metrics and losses.
    name: can be one of Loss or Accuracy
    """
    fig, ax = plt.subplots() #Creating a subplot with figure and axes.
    plt.plot(history.history[name]) #Plotting the train accuracy or train loss
    plt.plot(history.history['val_'+name]) #Plotting the validation accuracy or validation loss

    plt.title('Model ' + name.capitalize()) #Defining the title of the plot.
    plt.ylabel(name.capitalize()) #Capitalizing the first letter.
    plt.xlabel('Epoch') #Defining the label for the x-axis.
    fig.legend(['Train', 'Validation'], loc="outside right upper") #Defining the legend, loc controls the position of the legend.
In [311]:
# defining a function to compute different metrics to check performance of a classification model built using statsmodels
def model_performance_classification(
    model, predictors, target, threshold=0.5
):
    """
    Function to compute different metrics to check classification model performance

    model: classifier
    predictors: independent variables
    target: dependent variable
    threshold: threshold for classifying the observation as class 1
    """

    # checking which probabilities are greater than threshold
    pred = model.predict(predictors) > threshold
    # pred_temp = model.predict(predictors) > threshold
    # # rounding off the above values to get classes
    # pred = np.round(pred_temp)

    acc = accuracy_score(target, pred)  # to compute Accuracy
    recall = recall_score(target, pred, average='macro')  # to compute Recall
    precision = precision_score(target, pred, average='macro')  # to compute Precision
    f1 = f1_score(target, pred, average='macro')  # to compute F1-score

    # creating a dataframe of metrics
    df_perf = pd.DataFrame(
        {"Accuracy": acc, "Recall": recall, "Precision": precision, "F1 Score": f1,}, index = [0]
    )

    return df_perf
In [312]:
# Calculate class weights for imbalanced dataset
cw = (y_train.shape[0]) / np.bincount(y_train)

# Create a dictionary mapping class indices to their respective class weights
cw_dict = {}
for i in range(cw.shape[0]):
    cw_dict[i] = cw[i]

cw_dict
Out[312]:
{0: np.float64(1.0587612493382743), 1: np.float64(18.01801801801802)}

Initial Model Building (Model 0) - ReLU & SGD¶

  • Let's start with a neural network consisting of
    • just one hidden layer of 14 neurons respectively
    • activation function of ReLU.
    • SGD as the optimizer
In [313]:
# defining the batch size and # epochs upfront as we'll be using the same values for all models
epochs = 25
batch_size = 64
In [314]:
# clears the current Keras session, resetting all layers and models previously created, freeing up memory and resources.
tf.keras.backend.clear_session()
In [315]:
#Initializing the neural network
model_0 = Sequential()
model_0.add(Dense(14,activation="relu",input_dim=X_train.shape[1]))
model_0.add(Dense(1,activation="sigmoid"))
In [316]:
model_0.summary()

Model: "sequential"

┏━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓
┃ Layer (type)                    ┃ Output Shape           ┃       Param # ┃
┡━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┩
│ dense (Dense)                   │ (None, 14)             │           574 │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ dense_1 (Dense)                 │ (None, 1)              │            15 │
└─────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────┴───────────────┘

 Total params: 589 (2.30 KB)

 Trainable params: 589 (2.30 KB)

 Non-trainable params: 0 (0.00 B)
In [317]:
optimizer = tf.keras.optimizers.SGD()   # defining SGD as the optimizer to be used
model_0.compile(loss='binary_crossentropy', optimizer=optimizer, metrics = ['Recall'])
In [318]:
start = time.time()
history = model_0.fit(X_train, y_train, validation_data=(X_val,y_val) , batch_size=batch_size, epochs=epochs, class_weight = cw_dict)
end=time.time()

Let's check the classification reports.

In [323]:
y_train_pred_0 = model_0.predict(X_train)
y_val_pred_0 = model_0.predict(X_val)

500/500 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 1s 1ms/step
125/125 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 0s 2ms/step
In [324]:
print("Classification Report - Train data Model_0",end="\n\n")
cr_train_model_0 = classification_report(y_train,y_train_pred_0>0.5)
print(cr_train_model_0)

Classification Report - Train data Model_0

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

         0.0       0.99      0.96      0.98     15112
         1.0       0.58      0.91      0.71       888

    accuracy                           0.96     16000
   macro avg       0.79      0.94      0.85     16000
weighted avg       0.97      0.96      0.96     16000
In [325]:
print("Classification Report - Validation data Model_0",end="\n\n")
cr_val_model_0 = classification_report(y_val,y_val_pred_0>0.5)
print(cr_val_model_0)

Classification Report - Validation data Model_0

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

         0.0       0.99      0.96      0.98      3778
         1.0       0.58      0.87      0.70       222

    accuracy                           0.96      4000
   macro avg       0.79      0.92      0.84      4000
weighted avg       0.97      0.96      0.96      4000

Observations on Model 0 Performance:¶

  • Strong Recall: Model 0 achieved a strong Recall for the critical minority Class 1 (Failure) (0.91 on training, 0.87 on validation), meeting the goal of minimizing False Negatives.

  • Good Generalization: Performance is consistent between training and validation sets (e.g., F1-score of 0.79 vs. 0.77), indicating no significant overfitting.

  • Effective Imbalance Handling: The use of class_weight successfully boosted minority class recall.

Model Performance Improvement¶

Model 1 - ReLU, SGD & Momentum¶

Let's update Model 1 by adding:

  • One layer of 14 neurons
  • Activation function Relu
  • Adding a momemtum of 0.9
In [326]:
# clears the current Keras session, resetting all layers and models previously created, freeing up memory and resources.
tf.keras.backend.clear_session()
In [327]:
#Initializing the neural network
model_1 = Sequential()
model_1.add(Dense(14,activation="relu",input_dim=X_train.shape[1]))
model_1.add(Dense(1,activation="sigmoid"))
In [328]:
model_1.summary()

Model: "sequential"

┏━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓
┃ Layer (type)                    ┃ Output Shape           ┃       Param # ┃
┡━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┩
│ dense (Dense)                   │ (None, 14)             │           574 │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ dense_1 (Dense)                 │ (None, 1)              │            15 │
└─────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────┴───────────────┘

 Total params: 589 (2.30 KB)

 Trainable params: 589 (2.30 KB)

 Non-trainable params: 0 (0.00 B)
In [329]:
optimizer = tf.keras.optimizers.SGD(momentum=0.9)    # defining SGD as the optimizer to be used
model_1.compile(loss='binary_crossentropy', optimizer=optimizer,  metrics=['Recall'])
In [330]:
start = time.time()
history = model_1.fit(X_train, y_train, validation_data=(X_val,y_val) , batch_size=batch_size, epochs=epochs, class_weight = cw_dict)
end=time.time()

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

         0.0       0.99      0.97      0.98     15112
         1.0       0.63      0.88      0.74       888

Classification Report - Validation data Model_1

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

         0.0       0.99      0.97      0.98      3778
         1.0       0.63      0.85      0.72       222

    accuracy                           0.96      4000
   macro avg       0.81      0.91      0.85      4000
weighted avg       0.97      0.96      0.97      4000

Observations on Model 1 Performance:¶

  • Reduction in Recall: Compared to Model 0, Model 1 had slightly lower recall in both training (0.88) and validation (0.85) for Class 1, suggesting that adding momentum wasn't as beneficial.

  • Good Generalization: The performance metrics are still consistent between the training and validation sets, suggesting good generalization without overfitting.

  • Epoch & Loss Function: Loss function fluctuated quite across all epochs and thus suggests that learning didn't improve overall

Model 2 - 2 Layers, ReLU & Adam¶

Let's add

  • One layer of 32 neurons
  • Second layer of 16 neurons
  • Adam optimizer with class weights
In [338]:
tf.keras.backend.clear_session()

#Initializing the neural network
model_2 = Sequential()
model_2.add(Dense(32,activation="relu",input_dim=X_train.shape[1])) # First hidden layer
model_2.add(Dense(16,activation="relu")) # Second hidden layer
model_2.add(Dense(1,activation="sigmoid")) # Output layer

model_2.summary()

optimizer = tf.keras.optimizers.Adam()   # defining Adam as the optimizer to be used
model_2.compile(loss='binary_crossentropy', optimizer=optimizer, metrics = ['Recall'])

start = time.time()
history = model_2.fit(X_train, y_train, validation_data=(X_val,y_val) , batch_size=batch_size, epochs=epochs, class_weight = cw_dict)
end=time.time()

print("Time taken in seconds ",end-start)

plot(history,'loss')

Model: "sequential"

┏━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓
┃ Layer (type)                    ┃ Output Shape           ┃       Param # ┃
┡━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┩

│ dense_1 (Dense)                 │ (None, 16)             │           528 │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ dense_2 (Dense)                 │ (None, 1)              │            17 │

y_val_pred_2 = model_2.predict(X_val)

125/125 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 0s 2ms/step
In [342]:

cr_train_model_2 = classification_report(y_train,y_train_pred_2>0.5)
print(cr_train_model_2)

Classification Report - Train data Model_2

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

         0.0       1.00      0.98      0.99     15112
         1.0       0.72      0.95      0.82       888

    accuracy                           0.98     16000
   macro avg       0.86      0.96      0.90     16000
weighted avg       0.98      0.98      0.98     16000
In [343]:
print("Classification Report - Validation data Model_2",end="\n\n")
cr_val_model_2 = classification_report(y_val,y_val_pred_2>0.5)
print(cr_val_model_2)

Classification Report - Validation data Model_2

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

         0.0       0.99      0.98      0.99      3778
         1.0       0.70      0.88      0.78       222

    accuracy                           0.97      4000
   macro avg       0.84      0.93      0.88      4000
weighted avg       0.98      0.97      0.97      4000

Observations on Model 2 performance:

  • Good Recall: Compared to previous models, Model 2 had slightly higher recall in both training (0.95) and validation (0.88) for Class 1, suggesting that using Adaptive moment helped in increasing recall.

  • Good Generalization: The performance metrics are still consistent between the training and validation sets, suggesting good generalization without overfitting.

  • Epoch & Loss Function: Loss function fluctuated quite a bit across all epochs for validation set but seems to have normalized for training set after 7 epods and thus suggests that learning didn't improve overall in the validation set

Model 3 - Adam & Regularization¶

Let add dropouts of 0.4 and 0.2 after each layer of neurons respectively

In [344]:
tf.keras.backend.clear_session()

#Initializing the neural network
model_3 = Sequential()
model_3.add(Dense(32,activation="relu",input_dim=X_train.shape[1])) # First hidden layer
model_3.add(Dropout(0.4))
model_3.add(Dense(16,activation="relu")) # Second hidden layer
model_3.add(Dropout(0.2))
model_3.add(Dense(1,activation="sigmoid")) # Output layer

model_3.summary()

optimizer = tf.keras.optimizers.Adam()   # defining Adam as the optimizer to be used
model_3.compile(loss='binary_crossentropy', optimizer=optimizer, metrics = ['Recall'])

start = time.time()
history = model_3.fit(X_train, y_train, validation_data=(X_val,y_val) , batch_size=batch_size, epochs=epochs, class_weight = cw_dict)
end=time.time()

print("Time taken in seconds ",end-start)

plot(history,'loss')

Model: "sequential"

Classification Report - Train data Model_3

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

         0.0       0.99      0.99      0.99     15112
         1.0       0.83      0.90      0.87       888

    accuracy                           0.98     16000
   macro avg       0.91      0.95      0.93     16000
weighted avg       0.99      0.98      0.98     16000
In [349]:

cr_val_model_3 = classification_report(y_val,y_val_pred_3>0.5)
print(cr_val_model_3)

Classification Report - Validation data Model_3

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

         0.0       0.99      0.99      0.99      3778
         1.0       0.86      0.89      0.87       222

    accuracy                           0.99      4000
   macro avg       0.93      0.94      0.93      4000
weighted avg       0.99      0.99      0.99      4000

Observations on Model 3 Performance:¶

  • Good Recall:

    • On the training data, Model 3 achieves a recall of 0.90 for class 1 (failure), which is slightly lower the the last model but comparable to Model 0 and Model 1.
    • On the validation data, the recall for class 1 is 0.89, which is similar to previous models.
    • The recall remains strong, indicating the model's effectiveness in identifying actual failures, and the dropout layers have not significantly hindered its ability to detect the minority class.

  • Generalization:

    • Model 3 shows excellent generalization, with its validation metrics (especially precision and F1-score) being as good as, or better than, its training metrics for the minority class. This indicates that the dropout layers were effective in regularizing the model and preventing overfitting.

  • Loss Function and Epochs:

    • The loss plot shows a slightly more stable validation loss compared to Model 2, which is expected with dropout regularization. The model continues to learn effectively within the 25 epochs, and the regularization seems to have provided a smoother training process.

Model 4 - BatchNormalization & Adam¶

  • Let's see if we can improve the validation recall even more
  • Let's add batch normalization to see whether we can stabilize the training process and thereby improve the scores.
In [350]:
# clears the current Keras session, resetting all layers and models previously created, freeing up memory and resources.
tf.keras.backend.clear_session()

#Initializing the neural network
model_4 = Sequential()
model_4.add(Dense(32,activation="relu",input_dim=X_train.shape[1]))
model_4.add(BatchNormalization())
model_4.add(Dense(16,activation="relu"))
model_4.add(BatchNormalization())
model_4.add(Dense(1,activation="sigmoid"))

model_4.summary()

optimizer = tf.keras.optimizers.Adam()    # defining Adam as the optimizer to be used
model_4.compile(loss='binary_crossentropy', optimizer=optimizer)

print("Time taken in seconds ",end-start)

plot(history,'loss')

Model: "sequential"

┏━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓

┡━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┩

end=time.time()

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

         0.0       0.99      0.98      0.99      3778
         1.0       0.74      0.89      0.81       222

    accuracy                           0.98      4000
   macro avg       0.86      0.94      0.90      4000
weighted avg       0.98      0.98      0.98      4000

#¶

Observations on Model 4 Performance:¶

  • High Recall: Model 4 has a comparably higher recall for the critical minority Class 1 (Failure) among most of models tested:

    • Training Recall: 0.94
    • Validation Recall: 0.90

  • Crucial Improvement: This significant gain confirms that the Batch Normalization (BN) layers effectively help the model identify more actual failures, strongly addressing the goal of minimizing False Negatives.

  • Stable Training: The addition of BN resulted in a smoother loss decrease and stable generalization (no significant overfitting).

Model 5 - BatchNormalization & Dropout¶

Let add the following to see if this combination improves the class imbalance problem we have even more:

  • BatchNormalization after both layers
  • Dropouts of 0.4 and 0.2 after each layer of neurons respectively
In [358]:
tf.keras.backend.clear_session()

#Initializing the neural network
model_5 = Sequential()
model_5.add(Dense(32,activation="relu",input_dim=X_train.shape[1])) # First hidden layer
model_5.add(BatchNormalization())
model_5.add(Dropout(0.4))
model_5.add(Dense(16,activation="relu")) # Second hidden layer
model_5.add(BatchNormalization())
model_5.add(Dropout(0.2))
model_5.add(Dense(1,activation="sigmoid")) # Output layer

model_5.summary()

optimizer = tf.keras.optimizers.Adam()   # defining Adam as the optimizer to be used
model_5.compile(loss='binary_crossentropy', optimizer=optimizer, metrics = ['Recall'])

start = time.time()
history = model_5.fit(X_train, y_train, validation_data=(X_val,y_val) , batch_size=batch_size, epochs=epochs, class_weight = cw_dict)
end=time.time()

print("Time taken in seconds ",end-start)

plot(history,'loss')

Model: "sequential"

         0.0       0.99      0.99      0.99     15112
         1.0       0.81      0.91      0.86       888

   macro avg       0.90      0.95      0.92     16000

Classification Report - Validation data Model_5

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

         0.0       0.99      0.99      0.99      3778
         1.0       0.81      0.89      0.85       222

    accuracy                           0.98      4000
   macro avg       0.90      0.94      0.92      4000
weighted avg       0.98      0.98      0.98      4000

Observations on Model 5 Performance:¶

  • Strong Balance (F1-Score): Model 5 achieves a strong, well-balanced performance, particularly on the validation set, with an F1-Score of 0.85. This is better than Models 0, 1, 2, and 4, showing a good compromise between minimizing both false negatives and false positives.

  • High Recall Maintained: It sustains a high Validation Recall of 0.89, which is critical for minimizing costly False Negatives (missed failures).

  • Improved Precision: Validation Precision is 0.81, which is an improvement over most earlier models (0, 1, 2, 4), indicating better success in reducing False Positives (inspection costs).

  • Excellent Generalization: The combined use of Batch Normalization (BN) and Dropout effectively stabilizes training and ensures very close alignment between training and validation metrics, confirming no significant overfitting.

  • Robust Training: The loss decrease is stable and consistent throughout training due to the regularization techniques.

Model 6 - Dropout and HE Normalization¶

  • Let's initialize the weights using He normal.
  • We'll also use only Dropout for regularization.
In [364]:
tf.keras.backend.clear_session()

#Initializing the neural network
model_6 = Sequential()
model_6.add(Dense(32,activation="relu", kernel_initializer="he_normal",input_dim=X_train.shape[1])) # First hidden layer
model_6.add(Dropout(0.4))
model_6.add(Dense(16,activation="relu", kernel_initializer="he_normal")) # Second hidden layer
model_6.add(Dropout(0.2))
model_6.add(Dense(1,activation="sigmoid", kernel_initializer="he_normal")) # Output layer

model_6.summary()

optimizer = tf.keras.optimizers.Adam()   # defining Adam as the optimizer to be used
model_6.compile(loss='binary_crossentropy', optimizer=optimizer, metrics = ['Recall'])

start = time.time()
history = model_6.fit(X_train, y_train, validation_data=(X_val,y_val) , batch_size=batch_size, epochs=epochs, class_weight = cw_dict)
end=time.time()

print("Time taken in seconds ",end-start)

plot(history,'loss')

Model: "sequential"

Let's check the classification reports.

y_val_pred_6 = model_6.predict(X_val)

Classification Report - Validation data Model_6

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

         0.0       0.99      0.99      0.99      3778
         1.0       0.82      0.88      0.85       222

    accuracy                           0.98      4000
   macro avg       0.90      0.94      0.92      4000
weighted avg       0.98      0.98      0.98      4000

Observations on Model 6 Performance:¶

  • Strong Balance (F1-Score): Model 6 achieves a strong, well-balanced performance, particularly on the validation set, with an F1-Score of 0.85. This is better than Models 0, 1, 2, and 4, showing a good compromise between minimizing both false negatives and false positives.

  • High Recall Maintained: It sustains a high Validation Recall of 0.88, which is critical for minimizing costly False Negatives (missed failures).

  • Improved Precision: Validation Precision is 0.82, which is an improvement over most earlier models (0, 1, 2, 4), indicating better success in reducing False Positives (inspection costs).

  • Excellent Generalization: The combined use of Batch Normalization (BN) and Dropout effectively stabilizes training and ensures very close alignment between training and validation metrics, confirming no significant overfitting.

  • Robust Training: The loss decrease is stable and consistent throughout training due to the regularization techniques.

Model Performance Comparison and Final Model Selection¶

Observations on Model 6 Performance:¶

  • Balanced Performance: Model 6 achieves a Validation F1-Score of 0.85, making it one of the most balanced models (similar to Model 5, slightly less than Model 3).

  • Strong Recall: Maintains high Validation Recall (0.88), ensuring few False Negatives (missed failures).

  • Good Precision: Achieves Validation Precision (0.82), effectively reducing False Positives (inspection costs) better than Models 0, 1, 2, and 4.

  • Excellent Generalization: Performance metrics are closely matched between training and validation, confirming no significant overfitting. The combination of He normal initialization and Dropout ensures a stable and robust learning process.

Now, in order to select the final model, we will compare the performances of all the models for the training and validation sets.

In [373]:
# training performance comparison

models_train_comp_df = pd.concat(
    [
        model_0_train_perf.T,
        model_1_train_perf.T,
        model_2_train_perf.T,
        model_3_train_perf.T,
        model_4_train_perf.T,
        model_5_train_perf.T,
        model_6_train_perf.T
    ],
    axis=1,
)
models_train_comp_df.columns = [
    "Model 0 - (SGD, Relu)",
    "Model 1 - ReLU, SGD & Momentum",
    "Model 2 - 2 Layers, ReLU & Adam",
    "Model 3 - Adam & Regularization",
    "Model 4 - BatchNormalization & Adam",
    "Model 5 - BatchNormalization & Dropout",
    "Model 6 - Dropout and HE Normalization"
]

# validation performance comparison

models_valid_comp_df = pd.concat(
    [
        model_0_val_perf.T,
        model_1_val_perf.T,
        model_2_val_perf.T,
        model_3_val_perf.T,
        model_4_valid_perf.T,
        model_5_val_perf.T,
        model_6_val_perf.T
    ],
    axis=1,
)
models_valid_comp_df.columns = [
    "Model 0 - (SGD, Relu)",
    "Model 1 - ReLU, SGD & Momentum",
    "Model 2 - 2 Layers, ReLU & Adam",
    "Model 3 - Adam & Regularization",
    "Model 4 - BatchNormalization & Adam",
    "Model 5 - BatchNormalization & Dropout",
    "Model 6 - Dropout and HE Normalization"
]

Model 0 - (SGD, Relu) 0.019178

Observations:

  • From the above tables, it seems like Model 3 - Adam & Regularization and Model 5 - BatchNormalization & Dropout are really close in performance. But when looking at the other metrics, Model 3 has better precision and F1 score and thus we move forward with Model 3

Now, let's check the performance of the final model on the test set.

In [378]:
# clears the current Keras session, resetting all layers and models previously created, freeing up memory and resources.
tf.keras.backend.clear_session()

#Initializing the neural network
final_model = Sequential()
final_model.add(Dense(32,activation="relu",input_dim=X_train.shape[1])) # First hidden layer
final_model.add(Dropout(0.4))
final_model.add(Dense(16,activation="relu")) # Second hidden layer
final_model.add(Dropout(0.2))
final_model.add(Dense(1,activation="sigmoid")) # Output layer

final_model.summary()

optimizer = tf.keras.optimizers.Adam()   # defining Adam as the optimizer to be used
final_model.compile(loss='binary_crossentropy', optimizer=optimizer, metrics = ['Recall'])

start = time.time()
history = final_model.fit(X_train, y_train, validation_data=(X_val,y_val) , batch_size=batch_size, epochs=epochs, class_weight = cw_dict)
end=time.time()

print("Time taken in seconds ",end-start)

plot(history,'loss')

Model: "sequential"

Observations:

  • Final selected models performs and generalizes well on the unsees test data
  • Class 1 recall is 0.87, F1-score is 85, which is very comparable to validation and training datasets
  • There is still scope to remediate the class imbalance and strive for even better recall scores for minority class

Actionable Insights and Recommendations¶

Actionable Insights:¶

  1. High Failure Detection (Recall): The selected Model 3, utilizing Adam optimizer and Dropout regularization, achieved a high recall of 0.89 on the validation data and 0.87 on the test data for detecting generator failures (Class 1). This is crucial as it signifies the model's strong ability to identify most actual failures, directly addressing the business objective of minimizing costly replacements (False Negatives).
  2. Balanced Performance (F1-Score): Model 3 also demonstrated a good F1-score of 0.87 on validation and 0.85 on test data. This indicates a solid balance between precision and recall, suggesting that while it effectively catches failures, it also maintains a reasonable rate of correctly identified non-failures, thus managing inspection costs.
  3. Generalizability: The performance metrics (Recall, Precision, F1-score) are consistent across training, validation, and unseen test datasets. This indicates that the model is robust and generalizes well to new, real-world sensor data, which is essential for its practical application in predictive maintenance.
  4. Effective Regularization: The use of dropout layers effectively prevented overfitting, ensuring that the model's strong performance is not merely due to memorizing the training data but learning generalizable patterns.
  5. Adam Optimizer Efficiency: The Adam optimizer, combined with regularization, proved more effective in achieving better overall performance metrics compared to simpler SGD or SGD with momentum, suggesting its suitability for this dataset's characteristics.

Business Recommendations:¶

  1. Implement Predictive Maintenance System: Integrate Model 3 into ReneWind's operational system to trigger alerts when a high probability of generator failure is predicted. This allows for proactive maintenance, repairing generators before they fail, which significantly reduces replacement costs.
  2. Focus on False Negatives Reduction: Given that replacement costs are significantly higher than repair costs, continuously monitor the model's recall for Class 1. While the current recall is strong, further improvements (e.g., through more advanced modeling techniques, data augmentation, or a higher threshold for intervention if business risk allows) should be explored to reduce missed failures to an absolute minimum.
  3. Optimize Inspection Protocols: The model's balanced F1-score suggests that it also manages False Positives well, meaning fewer unnecessary inspections. ReneWind can use the model's predictions to optimize inspection schedules, focusing resources on units with a higher predicted risk of failure rather than routine checks, thereby lowering inspection costs.
  4. Continuous Monitoring and Retraining: Regularly monitor the model's performance in real-world scenarios. As environmental factors or turbine characteristics change over time, the model may need to be retrained with new data to maintain its predictive accuracy and recall. Establish a pipeline for continuous model evaluation and retraining.
  5. Data Quality and Feature Engineering: While the current dataset is ciphered, exploring additional sensor data or engineering new features from existing data could potentially uncover stronger predictive signals, leading to further improvements in failure prediction. This can be done in collaboration with the data collection teams while maintaining data confidentiality.
